analogy: Think of "Our Changing Earth" through the chapter's own opening images: Place the beaker on a tripod stand and heat it. As the water warms up, you will observe that the paper pellet is moving upward along with the warm layers of water and then sinks back along with the cooler layers of water. The molten... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Our Changing Earth is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that place the beaker on a tripod stand and heat it.

The text explains that as the water warms up, you will observe that the paper pellet is moving upward along with the warm layers of water and then sinks back along with the cooler layers of water.

The text explains that the molten magma inside the earth moves in a similar manner.

The text explains that the lithosphere is broken into a number of plates known as the Lithospheric plates.

The text explains that you will be surprised to know that these plates move around very slowly – just a few millimetres each year.

The text explains that this is because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth.

The text explains that the molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner as shown in the activity.

The text explains that the movement of these plates causes changes on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that the earth movements are divided on the basis of the forces which cause them.

The text explains that the forces which act in the interior of the earth are called as Endogenic forces and the forces that work on the surface of the earth are called as Exogenic forces (Fig.

The text explains that 3 Our Changing Earth Glossary Lithospheric plates: The earth’s crust consists of several large and some small, rigid, irregularly- shaped plates (slabs) which carry continents and the ocean floor.

The text explains that 3.1: Evolution of Landforms Earth movements Endogenic forces Exogenic forces Sudden forces Diastrophic forces Erosional and Depositional Building mountains Earthquake Volcano Landslides River Wind Sea-waves Glaciers Endogenic forces sometimes produce sudden movements and at the other times produce slow movements.

The text explains that sudden movements like earthquakes and volcanoes cause mass destruction over the surface of the earth.

The text explains that crater Lava tube Vent Lava Gases and ash Crust Mantle Magma Chamber Word Origin Fig.

The text explains that 3.2: A Volcano Epicentre Seismic waves radiate out in all directions Plate boundary Focus – this is where the earthquake occurs.

The text explains that it is the origin of the seismic energy A volcano is a vent (opening) in the earth’s crust through which molten material erupts suddenly (Fig.

The text explains that 3.3: Origin of an Earthquake Activity Take a container, fill it with water and close it with a lid.

The text explains that in the same manner, the earth vibrates when an earthquake occurs.

The text explains that there are three types of earthquake waves: 1.

The text explains that l waves or surface waves Try to find out the properties of these waves from an encyclopedia.

The text explains that these vibrations are called earthquakes (Fig.

The text explains that the place in the crust where the movement starts is called the focus.

The text explains that vibrations travel outwards from the epicentre as waves.

The text explains that greatest damage is usually closest to the epicentre and the strength of the earthquake decreases away from the centre.

The text explains that an earthquake is measured with a machine called a seismograph.

The text explains that the magnitude of the earthquake is measured on the Richter scale.

The text explains that an earthquake of 2.0 or less can be felt only a little.

The text explains that an earthquake over 5.0 can cause damage from things falling.

The text explains that a 6.0 or higher magnitude is considered very strong and 7.0 is classified as a major earthquake.

The text explains that a Seismograph EARTHQUAKE PREPAREDNESS Where to take shelter during an earthquake — Safe Spot – Under a kitchen counter, table or desk, against an inside corner or wall.

The text explains that 3.3a: Destruction caused by an Earthquake in Gujarat 1.

The text explains that read the ‘Earthquake – A case study’ given in the form of headlines that appeared in the newspapers after the quake.

The text explains that although earthquakes cannot be predicted, the impact can certainly be minimised if we are prepared before-hand.

The text explains that some common earthquake prediction methods adopted locally by people include studying animal behaviour; fish in the ponds get agitated, snakes come to the surface.

The text explains that eARTHQUAKE HITS BHUJ A massive earthquake measuring 6.9 on Richter scale hit Bhuj Town on 26th January 2001.

The text explains that weathering is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth’s surface.

The text explains that when the river tumbles at steep angle over very hard rocks or down a steep valley side it forms a waterfall (Fig.

The text explains that the Niagra falls Hard rock Softer rock undercut by the power of the water Fig.

The text explains that work of Sea Waves The erosion and deposition of the sea waves gives rise to coastal landforms.

The text explains that thus, hollow like caves are formed on the rocks.

The text explains that the steep rocky coast rising almost vertically above sea water is called sea cliff.

The text explains that the sea waves deposit sediments along the shores forming beaches.

The text explains that crescent shaped lake formed by a meandering river 10.

The text explains that fine sand deposited by the action of the wind 13.

The text explains that isolated mass of rising steep rock near a coastline 14.

The text explains that alluvial tracts of land formed by the river deposits at the mouth of a river Across 2.

The text explains that sudden descent of water in the bed of a river 11.

The text explains that natural cavity on weak rocks formed by action of waves 12.

The text explains that embankment on a river that keeps it in its channel 13.

The text explains that small hill of sand caused by the action of the wind 16.

The text explains that flat plain formed by river deposits during time of flood 6.

The text explains that solve the crossword puzzle with the help of given clues.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 3: Our Changing Earth

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3 Our Changing Earth and 13 OUR CHANGING EARTH as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Our Changing Earth; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Our Changing Earth through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3 Our Changing Earth and 13 OUR CHANGING EARTH to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
